agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider 2025 Homeless Count results for Vancouver
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will review a report and presentation on the 2025 Homeless Count for Vancouver. This is an information item only — no bylaw or regulation is being proposed for a vote. The count provides data on homelessness in the region, which could inform future policy decisions that may affect businesses, such as zoning or social service planning. No speakers are allowed during this agenda item per the Procedure Bylaw.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
1.	2025 Homeless Count – Vancouver
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
Presentation
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Cultural Grants for Operating & Spaces
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on proposed 2026 Cultural Grants that would provide funding for arts and culture organizations in Burnaby. The grants cover two areas: operating costs and cultural spaces. Because some of the recommendations require a special two-thirds majority vote under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter (a rule that demands extra support from council members for certain decisions), this is not a done deal yet. The decision is still upcoming, and the outcome depends on the council vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote on these grants — if approved, they could affect funding for your arts or cultural organization.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
2.	2026 Cultural Grants (Balance of Operating, Cultural Spaces)
Recommendations A to C, E and G require at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants for Spring 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to award grants to community sport events happening in Vancouver during the spring of 2026. This is part of the Vancouver Community Sport Event Grant program. The recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote 'yes' to pass, as per Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter (a special rule for certain council decisions). If approved, the grants would help local organizations host sporting events that benefit the community.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you run a community sport event in Vancouver, watch for the council decision — this could mean grant funding for your spring 2026 event.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
3.	Vancouver Community Sport Event Grants – Spring 2026 Intake
Recommendation A requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby property tax distribution for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on how to divide the total property tax levy among different classes of properties (like homes, businesses, and industry) for the 2026 tax year. This proposed decision will determine how much of the overall tax burden falls on commercial and industrial properties versus residential properties. A change in the distribution could mean a significant increase or decrease in your property tax bill, depending on your property class. The vote is part of the upcoming budget approval process.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision and the final 2026 budget — this will directly affect your property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development
agenda_briefs.source_body:
4.	2026 Property Taxation: Distribution of Property Tax Levy
4a. Budget Resolution
4b. Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 property tax exemptions for non-profits
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a report that lists which non-profit organizations would get a break on their 2025 property taxes. A proposed bylaw would exempt certain properties from paying property tax, which lowers costs for eligible groups like charities and community services. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on the list at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and check if your non-profit is on the proposed exemption list.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
5.	2025 Property Tax Exemptions
(no speakers per section 7.2(d) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Coastal Flood Management Policy
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed Coastal Flood Management Policy. This policy would create new rules for building and development in areas of Burnaby that are at risk of flooding from the ocean and rising sea levels. If approved, property owners in these flood-risk zones may face new requirements for building elevations, flood-proofing, and drainage. The goal is to protect people and property from future flood damage. The policy is still a proposal and has not been finalized.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Check if your property is in a proposed flood-risk zone and review the draft policy before the council vote.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
6.	Coastal Flood Management Policy
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board Annual Report 2025
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a recommendation (Recommendation B) regarding the Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board's Annual Report for 2025. This recommendation requires at least two-thirds of all council members to vote in favour, as stated under Section 206(1) of the Vancouver Charter. The Vancouver Charter is the city's main governing law. This is a proposed decision that has not yet been approved.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.	Vancouver Heritage Foundation Board – Annual Report 2025
Recommendation B requires at least 2/3 affirmative votes of all Council members under Section 206(1) of theVancouver Charter
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on 2026–2029 public plaza alcohol zones
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed program that would allow people to drink alcohol in certain public plazas in Burnaby from 2026 to 2029. The report recommends specific sites where this would be allowed. This is not yet approved — the decision is upcoming. If passed, the program would let licensed businesses (like nearby restaurants or bars) serve alcohol in these outdoor public spaces during certain hours. Business owners who operate in or near these plazas should pay attention, as it could affect their liquor licence or create new foot traffic opportunities.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — if approved, you may need to apply for a permit to serve alcohol in the plaza near your business.

key_dates.label: Council vote on 2026–2029 public plaza alcohol program

agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.	Sites for 2026-2029 Alcohol Consumption in Public Plazas Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to apply for development approvals funding
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on whether to apply for a provincial grant through the Local Government Development Approvals Program. If approved, the funding would help the city speed up its review and approval process for new development projects. This could mean faster decisions on building permits and rezoning applications for local businesses and developers. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on the application at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, this could speed up your future development applications in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.	Funding Application to Local Government Development Approvals Program
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on housing rezoning rule changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a package of miscellaneous amendments that would change the conditions attached to various housing rezoning approvals in Burnaby. These proposed changes could affect what developers and property owners must do — such as providing community amenities, affordable units, or public benefits — before a rezoning is finalized. The exact details of the amendments are not yet public, but the decision could impact future housing projects and related costs for builders and landlords. The item is on the council agenda and has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision and review the full amendment details once released — this could affect your rezoning application or property development costs.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
10.	Miscellaneous Amendments Concerning Various Housing Rezoning Conditions
(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Text amendment to CD-1 (839) bylaw for 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed text amendment to the Comprehensive Development (CD-1) Bylaw No. 13493 for the properties at 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue. A CD-1 bylaw is a special zoning rule that applies only to a specific site. This amendment would change the rules for what can be built or done on these two properties. The decision has not been made yet — council will consider the draft bylaw at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend the public hearing to have your say on this proposed zoning change.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.	CD-1 (839) Text Amendment: 5590 Victoria Drive and 2005 East 40th Avenue
(no speakers per section 7.2(b) of the Procedure By-law)
Report

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning at 4088 Granville Street, Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning bylaw for 4088 Granville Street. The proposal would change the property's zoning to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) district, which is a special zoning category that allows for a specific development plan. This is not yet approved — Council will consider the draft bylaw at an upcoming meeting. If passed, the rezoning would permit a new development on the site, but the exact details of what will be built are not yet final.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to speak for or against the rezoning proposal.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning for 3803-3823 West 10th Ave and 2553 Highbury St
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed rezoning for properties at 3803-3823 West 10th Avenue and 2553 Highbury Street. If approved, the project would change the land-use rules (called a CD-1 rezoning) to allow a new development on these sites. The proposal also includes changes to the Sign By-law. This is not yet approved — the decision is upcoming.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision date — this rezoning could affect nearby property values and development plans.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: CD-1 Rezoning for 310 East 14th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the property at 310 East 14th Avenue. This is called a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) rezoning, which means the city would create a special set of rules just for this site, instead of following the standard zoning rules. The draft bylaw to amend the Zoning and Development Bylaw is up for approval at the next meeting. No public speakers are allowed at this stage under the Procedure Bylaw.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this rezoning could affect nearby property values and development plans.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the properties at 1305-1325 West 13th Avenue. A 'rezoning' means changing the official rules about what can be built or done on that land. This is a first step — the draft bylaw is up for approval at the next council meeting. If passed, it would amend the city's main Zoning and Development Bylaw. No public speakers are scheduled for this item under the council's procedure rules.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this rezoning could affect nearby property values or development plans.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby amenity cost charge bylaw and density bonus updates
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to consider proposed updates to the Amenity Cost Charge By-law and Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning rules. These changes would affect how developers pay for community amenities (like parks, community centres, and childcare facilities) when building new projects. The report outlines required public consultation before any final decision is made. A proposed bylaw would set new rates and rules for these charges, which could impact the cost and feasibility of development projects in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public consultation period and attend any open houses or hearings to have your say on the proposed changes.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
16.	2026 Financing Growth Update – Required Consultation for Amenity Cost Charge By law and Bill 16 Density Bonus and Inclusionary Zoning Updates
Report
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw to assess Trounce Alley & Blood Alley Square maintenance costs
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would charge property owners in the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square area for the 2025 maintenance costs of those public spaces. This is a 'local improvement' project, meaning the costs are shared among the properties that directly benefit from the upkeep. The bylaw would allow the city to collect money from these property owners to cover things like cleaning, repairs, and landscaping for the alley and square. The decision has not been made yet — council will consider and vote on this bylaw at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own property in the Trounce Alley or Blood Alley Square area, watch for the council vote — this could affect your property taxes.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Food Service
agenda_briefs.source_body: 8.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 maintenance costs for the Trounce Alley and Blood Alley Square Local Improvement Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Bylaw to assess property costs for East Hastings parking project
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would assess (charge) property owners for the 2025 costs of the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project. This means the City would collect money from property owners in a specific area to help pay for shared parking improvements. The bylaw has not been approved yet — it is up for discussion and a vote at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit comments if you own property on East Hastings Street.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development
agenda_briefs.source_body: 7.	A By-law to assess real property to defray 2025 costs for the East Hastings Street Collective Parking Project
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Housing agreement bylaw for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would create a Housing Agreement for the property at 320-360 West 2nd Avenue. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that locks in certain conditions — like how many rental units must be kept affordable, for how long, and who can live there. This agreement is not yet in effect; Council will decide whether to approve it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this agreement could affect rental rules and property values in the area.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 6.	A By-law to enact a Housing Agreement for 320-360 West 2nd Avenue
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on amending housing agreement for 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Ave & 1002 Station St
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend an existing Housing Agreement for three properties: 450-496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue, and 1002 Station Street. A Housing Agreement is a legal contract between the city and a property owner that sets rules about how the housing must be operated — for example, keeping rents affordable or limiting who can live there. This proposed amendment would change the terms of that agreement. The decision has not been made yet; Council will consider the bylaw at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit comments if you are a tenant or owner affected by this housing agreement.
agenda_briefs.source_body: 5.	A By-law to authorize the amendment of a Housing Agreement Authorized by By-law No. 14507 (450 – 496 Prior Street, 550 Malkin Avenue and 1002 Station Street)
agenda_briefs.article_title: Revised Regular Council Revised Agenda — April 14, 2026

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on zoning amendment at 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change the zoning rules for a property at 1700-1750 West 75th Avenue. This is an amendment to an existing Comprehensive Development (CD-1) bylaw, which is a special zoning plan for a specific site. If approved, the change would allow different types of development or uses on that land. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on it at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and attend or submit comments if you own or operate a business on or near this property.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezoning of 1710-1730 East Pender Street to CD-1
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the properties at 1710-1730 East Pender Street to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. A CD-1 zone is a special zoning category that allows for a specific development plan, rather than following the standard rules for that area. This means the land could be used for a project that needs its own set of rules. The decision has not been made yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Attend the public hearing or submit your comments to Council before the vote to have your say on this rezoning.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Rezone 6486 Chester Street to CD-1 in Burnaby
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone the property at 6486 Chester Street to a Comprehensive Development (CD-1) zone. A CD-1 zone is a custom zoning category that allows the city to set specific rules for a particular property, rather than following the standard zoning rules for that area. This means the owner or developer of this property would need to follow a unique set of rules for what can be built or done there. Council will consider this proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: If you own or plan to develop property near 6486 Chester Street, watch for the council decision — this rezoning could affect your property's value or development options.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

(Councillors Bligh, Fry and Mayor Sim will need to review the proceedings to be eligible to vote)

A 'CD-1' (Comprehensive Development) zone would replace the current zoning, allowing a specific development plan for this site. This is a referral report, meaning council is considering whether to move forward with public hearings and further review. The decision has not been made yet.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Fast-track rental housing and small hotels in new Burnaby mixed-use district
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposal to create a new 'Mixed-Use Commercial District' that would fast-track approvals for rental housing and small hotels. If approved, the new rules would allow developers to build rental apartments and small hotels (under a certain size) more quickly, without needing a full rezoning process for each project. This is a proposed change — it has not been approved yet. Council will consider the report at an upcoming meeting, and there may be a public hearing before a final vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to tell Council if you support or oppose the new district rules.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Landlord & Property Mgmt
